essay_text:
When I was young, my mother noticed how much I enjoyed searching for wildlife in the streets of Taipei, often
stopping to observe the ants that trailed the sidewalks after a day's expedition. Seeing the interest that I had in
animals, she began taking me on night walks around the garden. Whether it was observing snails slowly climbing
through the dewy grass after a light rain or listening to the croaks of mating frogs, I was—and still am—engrossed
in these little animal behaviors. Night walks with my mother around the garden taught me to observe the small
details of the world.
W
As a kid, I liked to produce sketches of exotic creatures I saw or read in books. Because I wanted my
drawings to be detailed, I learned to notice the many features that various animals possessed, such as the parietal
eye on the top of a lizard's head. Through my drawings, I tried to capture the beauty of Moltrecht's Green Tree
Frog with its rainbow-colored underbelly, and also discovered the hinged design of a box turtle's shell, which I later
E
found out provides a tight seal to defend against predators.
My father realized that animals turned my quiet self into a talker. Because of this, he would take our whole
family to the shores of Yehliu, Taiwan to search for hermit crabs and fish on the weekends that he was home. My
I
father and I are both quite reserved; however, we both love to observe wildlife and can spend hours talking about
the rare endemic species seen during our Vmorning hikes through the Elephant mountain that lies beyond our
house. Because my father is only able to return home to Taiwan on the weekends, these conversations mean all
the more to me.
E
To this day, I enjoy spending time in nature and talking to anyone I can about animals. In recent years, I have
enjoyed hiking with my biology teacher and classmates in the mountains beyond our school to collect scientific
data on our surroundings, such as the diversity of the fish population and the carbon dioxide concentration in the
streams. While trekking along the trails, I talked with my teacher about the fishing spots that I have been to or
R
shared facts with my classmates about the birds that we saw. By sharing my knowledge about animals, I am able
to strike up conversations with others, which gives me opportunities to get to know people better. Through these
experiences, I have come to recognize nature as something I want to preserve because it provides a place where I
can immerse myself and interact with new people. On occasions, I would revisit the locations I have been to, only
P
to realize that the forests I enjoyed exploring as a child had been replaced by houses and factories. This prompted
me to deeply consider what I can do for the local wildlife, and to a greater extent, wildlife around the world.
In the future, I want to channel my passion for animals, and also my recent, growing interest in computer
science, into preserving the wildlife diversity that exists today. In my high school computer science classes, I
realized how easy it is for computers to model problems in many fields. Through the understanding I have gained
and will learn in the future, I hope to bring forth an interesting dynamic between animals and computer science
with others in college and beyond. One day, I hope to work towards protecting Taiwan's wildlife through ways such
as bettering land management by analyzing species distribution and spreading awareness of these beautiful
species. I want to preserve the island of Formosa so that other people can also experience the happiness I found
in the nights I spent looking for city wildlife with my mother and the fishing trips I went on with my father.
8
prompt: Some students have a background, identity, interest, or talent that is so meaningful they believe their application would be incomplete without it. If this sounds like you, then please share your story.
persona_prompt: You are a writer who crafts reflective and earnest narratives, weaving together personal anecdotes with broader aspirations. Your writing style is grounded in precise observations and specific details, often linking childhood experiences to current interests and future goals. You use clear, descriptive language, maintaining a warm and appreciative tone, while occasionally employing cause-and-effect phrasing to explain motivations and connections.
sentence_style: Varied; often beginning with shorter, direct statements followed by more complex sentences that elaborate with subordinate clauses and descriptive phrases. Sentences flow logically, connecting ideas with conjunctions (e.g., "whether...or," "because," "while trekking," "by sharing...which gives"), creating a smooth, unhurried rhythm.
vocabulary_level: Moderate/Sophisticated — The writer uses precise, often scientific, terminology (e.g., "parietal eye," "endemic species," "carbon dioxide concentration") seamlessly within accessible, descriptive prose, balancing technical accuracy with narrative clarity.
structural_patterns: Chronological progression from childhood influences to current engagements and future aspirations. The writer uses personal anecdotes as foundational evidence for evolving interests and values, often dedicating paragraphs to distinct periods or influential figures. The narrative follows a clear 'past-to-present-to-future' arc, culminating in a statement of purpose.
distinctive_moves: Starts paragraphs with clear topic sentences that often establish a timeframe or new influence (e.g., "When I was young," "As a kid," "My father realized," "To this day," "In the future").
Connects personal observations and experiences directly to larger lessons or insights (e.g., "taught me to observe the small details," "recognized nature as something I want to preserve").
Uses specific, often scientific, details to support descriptions (e.g., "parietal eye on the top of a lizard's head," "rainbow-colored underbelly," "carbon dioxide concentration in the streams").
Employs "cause and effect" reasoning to explain development or motivation ("Because I wanted... I learned," "This prompted me to deeply consider").
Refers to specific geographic locations (Taipei, Yehliu, Elephant mountain, Formosa) to ground experiences in a tangible, culturally specific setting.
themes_and_preoccupations: The transformative power of nature and detailed observation.
The profound influence of family in nurturing passions and values.
Connecting personal interests to broader societal and environmental concerns (e.g., wildlife conservation).
The intersection of seemingly disparate fields (natural sciences and computer science) for problem-solving.
The importance of preserving natural heritage for future generations.
self_presentation: The writer presents as a passionate and earnest observer, deeply reflective about their personal journey and the influences that shaped them. They are humble, yet clearly ambitious and purposeful, articulating a strong desire to contribute meaningfully to the world based on their experiences and evolving skills. There's an underlying sense of quiet determination and appreciation.
cultural_or_contextual_markers: Strong ties to Taiwan (e.g., Taipei, Yehliu, Elephant mountain, Formosa, specific mention of 'Taiwan's wildlife'). References to local wildlife, 'night walks,' and concerns about local deforestation ('forests I enjoyed exploring as a child had been replaced by houses and factories') firmly root the narrative in a specific geographic and environmental context.
avoid_in_imitation: Overly simplistic or overtly academic/jargon-heavy language that lacks the essay's balance of detail and accessibility.
Excessive use of rhetorical questions, abstract metaphors, or ironic/sarcastic tones.
A detached or overly confident/boastful presentation; avoid minimizing the role of others or external influences.
sample_sentence: From the intricate patterns on a butterfly's wing to the subtle shift in a river's current, every small detail has always sparked my curiosity, prompting me to look deeper into the world's hidden complexities and consider how I might help protect them.
